script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Wednesday — here is your QQQ recap. The portfolio closed down zero point four seven percent on the session. A mixed day for technology, with some names catching a bid while the broader complex pulled back into earnings season.

Alphabet reported after the close. Earnings crushed estimates. The stock fell anyway. That sets up a complicated open tomorrow for one of the portfolio's largest positions.

The Nasdaq closed lower on Wednesday as software stocks weighed on the index while semiconductor names provided some offset. Rising oil prices and Treasury yields kept investors cautious ahead of a heavy earnings slate, with market sentiment described as volatile despite ongoing artificial intelligence optimism. No major Federal Reserve commentary shaped Wednesday's session.

Alphabet's second quarter results headlined the after-hours session, and the numbers were genuinely strong. The company reported adjusted earnings of nine dollars and eleven cents per share against a Wall Street consensus of two dollars and eighty-eight cents per share, according to FactSet — a substantial beat driven in part by six dollars and twenty-six cents per share in gains on equity securities. Cloud computing growth accelerated, marking the company's twelfth consecutive quarter of solid revenue expansion. Search performance came in line with expectations. The problem is capital expenditure. Alphabet raised its spending forecast again, and investors sold the stock in extended hours trading. For a position that represents six point one eight percent of this portfolio, the after-hours move creates a real headwind for Thursday's open, even against an otherwise strong fundamental print.

Turning to the holdings rundown, starting at the top. Nvidia was up two point three zero percent on the session, making it one of the standout performers in the portfolio on Wednesday. The company was named a founding partner in Project Prometheus, a three-year, sixty-million-dollar initiative led by Idaho National Laboratory alongside Amazon Web Services to accelerate advanced nuclear deployment through artificial intelligence. The partnership targets long-term schedule acceleration and cost reduction in the nuclear sector. Nvidia's involvement deepens its footprint in AI-for-infrastructure applications well beyond data centers. Watch for any additional announcements tied to the Department of Energy's Genesis Mission program.

Apple was down zero point five six percent on the session. Reports circulated Wednesday that updated MacBook Pro, MacBook Air, and desktop models are in development, targeting artificial intelligence demand. Separately, commentary surfaced flagging valuation as an underappreciated risk for the stock at current levels. Apple reports earnings later this cycle — that will be the next major inflection point.

Alphabet was down one point four six percent during the regular session. As covered in the top story, the after-hours earnings picture is more complex — a strong beat on profits but elevated capital expenditure guidance that pressured the stock. Watch Thursday's open closely given the size of this position.

Micron was down one point one seven percent on the session. No company-specific news drove the move. Micron tracked general semiconductor softness in the session, though the broader chip space showed divergence, with memory names underperforming relative to graphics-focused peers. The next earnings report remains the key catalyst on the horizon.

Microsoft was down one point eight six percent on the session. The stock pulled back alongside broader software sector weakness, which was a notable drag on the Nasdaq during Wednesday's session. Microsoft reports earnings this cycle — that event will draw direct attention to cloud growth and artificial intelligence monetization.

Amazon was down one point zero nine percent on the session. No company-specific headlines emerged, but the stock tracked consumer discretionary and cloud-adjacent softness. Amazon's participation in the Project Prometheus nuclear and artificial intelligence initiative alongside Nvidia and Idaho National Laboratory is worth noting as a secondary data point for long-term positioning.

Advanced Micro Devices was up one point four five percent on the session. The stock moved in line with semiconductor strength Wednesday, benefiting from the same chip sector tailwind that lifted Nvidia. No company-specific news drove the move. Earnings from major peers this week will shape the near-term setup for Advanced Micro Devices.

Tesla was down one point three zero percent during the regular session, then slid further in extended hours after reporting mixed second quarter results. Adjusted earnings came in at thirty-three cents per share against estimates of fifty-one cents, while revenue of twenty-eight point two four billion dollars topped the twenty-six point three two billion dollar consensus. Negative cash flow was flagged as a concern by analysts. The stock's after-hours decline adds pressure to a position that already sits near the bottom of the primary holdings by weight.

Intel was down two point six eight percent on the session, making it the weakest primary holding on Wednesday. No specific news emerged, but Intel continues to underperform the broader semiconductor space on a relative basis. The stock remains a position to monitor closely given its persistent laggard status in an otherwise active chip environment.

Three items to watch in the next twenty-four to forty-eight hours. First, Tesla's post-earnings conference call and analyst commentary following Wednesday's mixed results — cash flow and margin trajectory will be the focus. Second, Alphabet's investor call and any further clarity on capital expenditure guidance, given the after-hours reaction. Third, the SpaceX Starship launch targeting July twenty-third — relevant context for the industrials exposure in this portfolio.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Watch Thursday's open closely — Alphabet and Tesla both have post-earnings price action to absorb, and the semiconductor names will need to hold their gains.
